1.1.1.name: Non-text Content
1.1.1.legalRelevance: Frequently cited in ADA/Section 508 complaints; critical for screen reader users
1.1.1.description: All non-text content has a text alternative that serves the equivalent purpose.

1.2.1.name: Audio-only and Video-only (Prerecorded)
1.2.1.legalRelevance: Required under Section 508; increasingly cited in ADA digital accessibility lawsuits
1.2.1.description: Prerecorded audio-only and video-only content has an alternative that presents equivalent information.

1.2.2.name: Captions (Prerecorded)
1.2.2.legalRelevance: High litigation risk; FCC regulations and ADA Title III require captions for public-facing video
1.2.2.description: Captions are provided for all prerecorded audio content in synchronized media.

1.2.3.name: Audio Description or Media Alternative (Prerecorded)
1.2.3.legalRelevance: Required under Section 508; essential for users who cannot see video content
1.2.3.description: An alternative for time-based media or audio description of the prerecorded video content is provided.

1.2.4.name: Captions (Live)
1.2.4.legalRelevance: Increasingly required for live events under ADA; FCC mandates for broadcast content
1.2.4.description: Captions are provided for all live audio content in synchronized media.

1.2.5.name: Audio Description (Prerecorded)
1.2.5.legalRelevance: Required under Section 508 and EU European Accessibility Act for prerecorded video content
1.2.5.description: Audio description is provided for all prerecorded video content in synchronized media.

1.3.1.name: Info and Relationships
1.3.1.legalRelevance: One of the most commonly failed WCAG criteria; frequently cited in ADA and Section 508 complaints
1.3.1.description: Information, structure, and relationships conveyed through presentation can be programmatically determined or are available in text.

1.3.2.name: Meaningful Sequence
1.3.2.legalRelevance: Important for Section 508 compliance; affects screen reader navigation order
1.3.2.description: When the sequence in which content is presented affects its meaning, a correct reading sequence can be programmatically determined.

1.3.3.name: Sensory Characteristics
1.3.3.legalRelevance: Cited in ADA complaints involving instructions that rely solely on shape, color, size, or location
1.3.3.description: Instructions provided for understanding and operating content do not rely solely on sensory characteristics such as shape, color, size, or visual location.

1.3.4.name: Orientation
1.3.4.legalRelevance: Required under EU European Accessibility Act; important for users with mounted devices
1.3.4.description: Content does not restrict its view and operation to a single display orientation unless a specific orientation is essential.

1.3.5.name: Identify Input Purpose
1.3.5.legalRelevance: Supports EU EAA compliance; benefits users with cognitive disabilities and those using autofill
1.3.5.description: The purpose of each input field collecting information about the user can be programmatically determined when the field serves a common purpose.

1.4.1.name: Use of Color
1.4.1.legalRelevance: Commonly cited in ADA and Section 508 complaints; affects users with color vision deficiencies
1.4.1.description: Color is not used as the only visual means of conveying information, indicating an action, prompting a response, or distinguishing a visual element.

1.4.2.name: Audio Control
1.4.2.legalRelevance: Required under Section 508; critical for screen reader users who need to hear their assistive technology
1.4.2.description: If any audio plays automatically for more than 3 seconds, a mechanism is available to pause, stop, or control the volume independently.

1.4.3.name: Contrast (Minimum)
1.4.3.legalRelevance: The single most commonly failed WCAG criterion per WebAIM Million; heavily cited in ADA litigation
1.4.3.description: Text and images of text have a contrast ratio of at least 4.5:1, except for large text which requires 3:1.

1.4.4.name: Resize Text
1.4.4.legalRelevance: Required under Section 508 and EU EAA; important for users with low vision
1.4.4.description: Text can be resized without assistive technology up to 200 percent without loss of content or functionality.

1.4.5.name: Images of Text
1.4.5.legalRelevance: Cited in ADA lawsuits involving image-based navigation and content; affects text resizing and translation
1.4.5.description: If the technologies being used can achieve the visual presentation, text is used to convey information rather than images of text.

1.4.10.name: Reflow
1.4.10.legalRelevance: Required under EU EAA; critical for users who zoom or use mobile devices
1.4.10.description: Content can be presented without loss of information or functionality and without requiring scrolling in two dimensions at 320 CSS pixels wide and 256 CSS pixels tall.

1.4.11.name: Non-text Contrast
1.4.11.legalRelevance: Increasingly cited in ADA complaints; applies to UI components and graphical objects
1.4.11.description: Visual information required to identify UI components and graphical objects has a contrast ratio of at least 3:1 against adjacent colors.

1.4.12.name: Text Spacing
1.4.12.legalRelevance: Required under EU EAA; supports users with dyslexia and low vision who need custom text spacing
1.4.12.description: No loss of content or functionality occurs when users override text spacing properties for line height, paragraph spacing, letter spacing, and word spacing.

1.4.13.name: Content on Hover or Focus
1.4.13.legalRelevance: Relevant to ADA and EU EAA compliance; affects tooltip and popup content accessibility
1.4.13.description: Where pointer hover or keyboard focus triggers additional content to become visible and then hidden, the additional content is dismissible, hoverable, and persistent.

2.1.1.name: Keyboard
2.1.1.legalRelevance: One of the most critical WCAG criteria for ADA and Section 508; essential for users who cannot use a mouse
2.1.1.description: All functionality of the content is operable through a keyboard interface without requiring specific timings for individual keystrokes.

2.1.2.name: No Keyboard Trap
2.1.2.legalRelevance: Frequently cited in ADA lawsuits; a keyboard trap can make a page completely unusable
2.1.2.description: If keyboard focus can be moved to a component using a keyboard interface, then focus can be moved away from that component using only a keyboard interface.

2.1.4.name: Character Key Shortcuts
2.1.4.legalRelevance: Required under EU EAA; prevents accidental activation for speech input and keyboard-only users
2.1.4.description: If a keyboard shortcut using only letter, punctuation, number, or symbol characters is implemented, then a mechanism is available to turn it off, remap it, or make it active only on focus.

2.2.1.name: Timing Adjustable
2.2.1.legalRelevance: Cited in ADA complaints involving session timeouts and timed content; affects users with cognitive disabilities
2.2.1.description: For each time limit set by the content, the user can turn off, adjust, or extend the time limit.

2.2.2.name: Pause, Stop, Hide
2.2.2.legalRelevance: Required under Section 508; critical for users with attention disorders and seizure conditions
2.2.2.description: For moving, blinking, scrolling, or auto-updating information, there is a mechanism for the user to pause, stop, or hide it.

2.3.1.name: Three Flashes or Below Threshold
2.3.1.legalRelevance: Critical safety requirement under ADA and Section 508; violations can trigger seizures in users with photosensitive epilepsy
2.3.1.description: Web pages do not contain anything that flashes more than three times in any one second period, or the flash is below the general flash and red flash thresholds.

2.4.1.name: Bypass Blocks
2.4.1.legalRelevance: Commonly cited in ADA and Section 508 complaints; skip navigation links are a standard requirement
2.4.1.description: A mechanism is available to bypass blocks of content that are repeated on multiple web pages.

2.4.2.name: Page Titled
2.4.2.legalRelevance: Frequently failed and cited in ADA complaints; essential for screen reader users to identify pages
2.4.2.description: Web pages have titles that describe topic or purpose.

2.4.3.name: Focus Order
2.4.3.legalRelevance: Cited in ADA and Section 508 audits; illogical focus order can make pages unusable for keyboard users
2.4.3.description: If a web page can be navigated sequentially and the navigation sequences affect meaning or operation, focusable components receive focus in an order that preserves meaning and operability.

2.4.4.name: Link Purpose (In Context)
2.4.4.legalRelevance: Among the most commonly failed criteria per WebAIM Million; heavily cited in ADA litigation
2.4.4.description: The purpose of each link can be determined from the link text alone or from the link text together with its programmatically determined link context.

2.4.5.name: Multiple Ways
2.4.5.legalRelevance: Required under Section 508 and EU EAA; supports users who navigate differently based on ability
2.4.5.description: More than one way is available to locate a web page within a set of web pages, except where the page is the result of or a step in a process.

2.4.6.name: Headings and Labels
2.4.6.legalRelevance: Cited in ADA and Section 508 complaints; proper headings are essential for screen reader navigation
2.4.6.description: Headings and labels describe topic or purpose.

2.4.7.name: Focus Visible
2.4.7.legalRelevance: Frequently cited in ADA lawsuits; removing focus indicators makes keyboard navigation impossible
2.4.7.description: Any keyboard operable user interface has a mode of operation where the keyboard focus indicator is visible.

2.4.11.name: Focus Not Obscured (Minimum)
2.4.11.legalRelevance: New in WCAG 2.2; addresses sticky headers and overlays that hide focused elements; relevant to EU EAA compliance
2.4.11.description: When a user interface component receives keyboard focus, the component is not entirely hidden due to author-created content.

2.5.1.name: Pointer Gestures
2.5.1.legalRelevance: Required under EU EAA; critical for users with motor disabilities who cannot perform complex gestures
2.5.1.description: All functionality that uses multipoint or path-based gestures can be operated with a single pointer without a path-based gesture, unless the gesture is essential.

2.5.2.name: Pointer Cancellation
2.5.2.legalRelevance: Required under EU EAA and Section 508; prevents accidental activation for users with motor impairments
2.5.2.description: For functionality operated using a single pointer, at least one of the following is true: the down-event is not used, completion is on the up-event with an abort/undo mechanism, or the up-event reverses the down-event.

2.5.3.name: Label in Name
2.5.3.legalRelevance: Important for ADA and Section 508 compliance; critical for speech input users who activate controls by speaking visible labels
2.5.3.description: For user interface components with labels that include text or images of text, the accessible name contains the text that is presented visually.

2.5.4.name: Motion Actuation
2.5.4.legalRelevance: Required under EU EAA; protects users with motor disabilities and those with mounted devices
2.5.4.description: Functionality that can be operated by device motion or user motion can also be operated by user interface components, and responding to the motion can be disabled to prevent accidental actuation.

2.5.7.name: Dragging Movements
2.5.7.legalRelevance: New in WCAG 2.2; required under EU EAA; ensures drag-and-drop interfaces are accessible to users with motor disabilities
2.5.7.description: All functionality that uses a dragging movement for operation can be achieved by a single pointer without dragging, unless dragging is essential or the functionality is determined by the user agent.

2.5.8.name: Target Size (Minimum)
2.5.8.legalRelevance: New in WCAG 2.2; required under EU EAA; addresses touch target issues frequently cited in mobile accessibility complaints
2.5.8.description: The size of the target for pointer inputs is at least 24 by 24 CSS pixels, except where an equivalent target exists, the target is inline, the size is user agent controlled, or the presentation is essential.

3.1.1.name: Language of Page
3.1.1.legalRelevance: Among the most commonly failed criteria per WebAIM Million; required for screen readers to use correct pronunciation
3.1.1.description: The default human language of each web page can be programmatically determined.

3.1.2.name: Language of Parts
3.1.2.legalRelevance: Required under Section 508 and EU EAA; ensures screen readers switch language pronunciation correctly
3.1.2.description: The human language of each passage or phrase in the content can be programmatically determined, except for proper names, technical terms, and words of indeterminate language.

3.2.1.name: On Focus
3.2.1.legalRelevance: Cited in ADA complaints; unexpected context changes on focus disorient users with cognitive and visual disabilities
3.2.1.description: When any user interface component receives focus, it does not initiate a change of context.

3.2.2.name: On Input
3.2.2.legalRelevance: Cited in ADA and Section 508 complaints; auto-submitting forms on input change causes usability issues
3.2.2.description: Changing the setting of any user interface component does not automatically cause a change of context unless the user has been advised of the behavior before using the component.

3.2.3.name: Consistent Navigation
3.2.3.legalRelevance: Required under Section 508 and EU EAA; supports users with cognitive disabilities who rely on consistent patterns
3.2.3.description: Navigational mechanisms that are repeated on multiple web pages within a set of web pages occur in the same relative order each time they are repeated.

3.2.4.name: Consistent Identification
3.2.4.legalRelevance: Required under Section 508 and EU EAA; inconsistent labeling confuses screen reader users and users with cognitive disabilities
3.2.4.description: Components that have the same functionality within a set of web pages are identified consistently.

3.2.6.name: Consistent Help
3.2.6.legalRelevance: New in WCAG 2.2; required under EU EAA; ensures users with cognitive disabilities can reliably find help mechanisms
3.2.6.description: If a web page contains help mechanisms that are repeated on multiple pages, they occur in the same relative order unless the user initiates a change.

3.3.1.name: Error Identification
3.3.1.legalRelevance: Frequently cited in ADA form accessibility complaints; critical for screen reader users to understand errors
3.3.1.description: If an input error is automatically detected, the item that is in error is identified and the error is described to the user in text.

3.3.2.name: Labels or Instructions
3.3.2.legalRelevance: Among the most commonly failed criteria per WebAIM Million; heavily cited in ADA and Section 508 lawsuits
3.3.2.description: Labels or instructions are provided when content requires user input.

3.3.3.name: Error Suggestion
3.3.3.legalRelevance: Required under Section 508 and EU EAA; important for form accessibility and users with cognitive disabilities
3.3.3.description: If an input error is automatically detected and suggestions for correction are known, then the suggestions are provided to the user unless it would jeopardize the security or purpose of the content.

3.3.4.name: Error Prevention (Legal, Financial, Data)
3.3.4.legalRelevance: Critical for ADA compliance on e-commerce and financial sites; protects users from irreversible legal/financial errors
3.3.4.description: For web pages that cause legal commitments or financial transactions, that modify or delete user-controllable data, or that submit test responses, submissions are reversible, checked, or confirmed.

3.3.7.name: Redundant Entry
3.3.7.legalRelevance: New in WCAG 2.2; required under EU EAA; reduces cognitive burden for users who find re-entering information difficult
3.3.7.description: Information previously entered by or provided to the user that is required to be entered again in the same process is either auto-populated or available for the user to select.

3.3.8.name: Accessible Authentication (Minimum)
3.3.8.legalRelevance: New in WCAG 2.2; required under EU EAA; critical for users with cognitive disabilities who struggle with memory-based authentication
3.3.8.description: A cognitive function test is not required for any step in an authentication process unless an alternative method or mechanism is available to assist the user.

4.1.2.name: Name, Role, Value
4.1.2.legalRelevance: One of the most critical criteria for ADA and Section 508; ensures custom components work with assistive technologies
4.1.2.description: For all user interface components, the name and role can be programmatically determined; states, properties, and values that can be set by the user can be programmatically set; and notification of changes is available to user agents including assistive technologies.

4.1.3.name: Status Messages
4.1.3.legalRelevance: Required under Section 508 and EU EAA; ensures screen reader users are informed of dynamic content updates without focus change
4.1.3.description: In content implemented using markup languages, status messages can be programmatically determined through role or properties such that they can be presented to the user by assistive technologies without receiving focus.
